mnemonics.title: OSI Layer Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: All People Seem To Need Data Processing - A(Pplication), P(resentation), S(ession), T(ransport), N(etwork), D(ata Link), P(hysical)

mnemonics.title: OSI Alternative Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away - P(hysical), D(ata Link), N(etwork), T(ransport), S(ession), P(resentation), A(pplication)

mnemonics.title: TCP/IP Layer Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Auntie Takes Ice Nectar - A(pplication), T(ransport), I(nternet), N(etwork Access/Link)

mnemonics.title: Network Types Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Personal Campus Metropolitan Wide - PAN (Personal), CAN (Campus), MAN (Metropolitan), WAN (Wide)

mnemonics.title: Topology Data Flow Order
mnemonics.content: Big Rats Make Huge Tree Holes - Bus, Ring, Mesh, Hybrid, Tree, Star

mnemonics.title: OSI PDU (Protocol Data Unit) Sequence
mnemonics.content: Data Packets Flow Down - Data → Segment → Packet → Frame → Bits (as it moves down OSI layers)

mnemonics.title: TCP/IP vs OSI Comparison
mnemonics.content: TCP/IP Has (4) Layers, OSI Has (7) - Remember: TCP/IP is simpler, OSI is more detailed teaching model

acronyms.usage: Reference model for network communication with 7 layers
Actual protocol suite used on the internet
Creates secure connection over public network
Data link layer addressing for network cards
Application layer protocol for web browsing

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a wheel with spokes - center is the hub/switch, computers are the spokes radiating outward. If one spoke breaks, other computers still work, but if hub fails, everything fails.
Think of a single highway road with houses (computers) on both sides. One main cable runs through, terminators at both ends to prevent signal bounce.
Imagine people sitting in a circle passing a message. Each person (computer) passes to the next. If one person leaves, the circle breaks.
Picture a spider web or fishing net - every node connects to multiple other nodes. Multiple paths exist for data to travel.
Visualize an organizational hierarchy or family tree. Main trunk (backbone) splits into branches, then into leaves (end devices).
Think of it as a 7-story building. Bottom floor (Physical) is foundation, top floor (Application) is where you interact. Data travels down from top to bottom, then across, then up.
Think of it as a 4-story building - simpler version of OSI. Application (top), Transport, Internet, Link (bottom). This is the actual implementation used today.
Imagine putting a letter inside an envelope, then that envelope inside another box, then that box on a truck. Each layer adds its own wrapper (header/trailer). At destination, unwrap layer by layer.

analogies.analogy: LAN is like your home electricity wiring (local, fast, yours). WAN is like the national power grid (spans large areas, more complex, shared infrastructure).

Think of sending a letter: You write message (Application), put in envelope with your address (Presentation), decide when to send (Session), ensure letter reaches correct person (Transport), determine route through postal system (Network), put in envelope with stamp (Data Link), actually hand to postman/carrier (Physical).
Like a telephone switchboard or airport control tower. All flights communicate through control tower; if tower fails, airport shuts down.
Like a school bus route on a single main road - one bus travels the route, picking up/dropping students at stops along the way.
Like a relay race where runners (data) pass the baton in a circle. Each runner can only pass to the next person in sequence.
Like the Indian railway network - multiple routes connect major cities. If one route has problems, trains can take alternative paths.
TCP is like a phone call - establishes connection, ensures complete message, orders packets, resends lost data. UDP is like sending a text message - just sends, doesn't check if received or in order.
MAC address is like your physical fingerprint - unique, permanent, built into device. IP address is like your postal address - can change, used for routing to location.
Hub is like a party line (old telephone) - when one person speaks, everyone hears, causes collisions. Switch is like a modern phone - creates direct connection between two parties, more efficient.
